current_fact.fact_text: On April 29, 1781, British and French fleets clashed off Martinique in the Battle of Fort Royal. As cannon smoke drifted over Caribbean waters, the fight was as much about who would control colonial trade and sea lanes as about flags and pride—a naval chess match played with creaking wooden giants.

episode.title: Morning Drops #23 - Morning Dubs
episode.description: Recorded while still processing Houghton Festival. One of the mornings there, waking up in the woods to a reggae DJ set — no phone signal, just trees, music, and loved ones. A slow selection of reggae, dub, and morning rhythms. Dubs instead of drops. Start your day slow — with space to breathe.
songs: The Gaylads - Joy in the Morning
King Tubby - King Tubby Meets the Rockers Uptown

current_fact.match_reason: A colonial naval battle in Caribbean waters contrasts with a peaceful, rootsy selection shaped by Caribbean music—reclaiming the same sea lanes with dub echo and morning light instead of cannon fire.

queue.fact_text: On April 29, 1483, the Kingdom of Castile completed its conquest of Gran Canaria, the main island of the Canary archipelago. The volcanic island, once home to the Indigenous Guanche people with their own language and culture, became a stepping stone for later voyages to the Americas and a testing ground for European colonial rule.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

queue.match_reason: The conquest of an island and its Indigenous people resonates with an episode centered on Tuareg music, exile, and resistance—amplifying voices shaped by colonization and survival in harsh landscapes.

queue.fact_text: On April 29, 1991, just hours after the Racha earthquake in Georgia, seismologists began mapping its intensity using the Medvedev–Sponheuer–Karnik scale, a European system that translates eyewitness descriptions into numbered degrees of shaking. Instead of just measuring raw energy, the scale encodes human experience: how many chimneys cracked, which walls fell, what people felt in their bones.

episode.title: Morning Drops #13 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

queue.match_reason: A scale that translates lived experience into structured degrees parallels an episode that breaks down reggaeton’s misunderstood history—both demystify complex phenomena by listening closely to people’s stories.

queue.fact_text: On April 29, 1991, meteorologists also revisited their models of the North Indian Ocean after the Bangladesh cyclone, refining warning systems for storm surges and wind fields. Each tragic data point feeds into better forecasts, slowly turning chaos into patterns that can give coastal communities a few more life‑saving hours.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: The painful lessons of a catastrophic storm leading to improved early warnings mirrors an intimate story of injury and slow recovery—both about looking back at damage to create gentler futures.

queue.fact_text: On April 29, 1991, far from Bangladesh and Georgia, TV viewers in Los Angeles who tuned into that 3D episode of “Space Patrol” joined a tiny experiment in human perception: could our brains be tricked into feeling depth from flat screens? The answer depended on a neat hack of binocular vision—each eye fed a slightly different picture, and the mind did the rest.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: A TV stunt that plays with human perception and screens connects to an episode about our psychological relationship with devices and digital overload, both probing how technology reshapes the way we see and feel.

queue.fact_text: On April 29, 1991, while cyclones and earthquakes dominated headlines, many people experienced these events only as images and sounds filtered through television and radio. In a sense, global disasters became shared stories carried by invisible waves in the air, turning planetary upheavals into something you could overhear while making dinner.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: The way broadcast media turns distant catastrophes into shared narratives fits the very first Sedna FM episode—a raw, foundational transmission that also travels as invisible waves, mixing world events, politics, and life into one signal.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1826, Scottish astronomer James Dunlop pointed his telescope toward the constellation Centaurus and logged a strange, hazy patch of light: the galaxy now known as Centaurus A, or NGC 5128. Today we know it’s a nearby radio-loud galaxy with a supermassive black hole shooting out colossal jets, but Dunlop saw only a ghostly smudge that hinted the universe was far richer than it seemed.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A distant, mysterious galaxy discovered as a faint "empty" patch mirrors the episode’s idea of seeing emptiness as creative space, with upbeat tracks fitting the cosmic, expansive theme.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1953, Los Angeles station KECA‑TV broadcast an experimental 3D episode of the sci‑fi series “Space Patrol,” making it the first U.S. experimental 3D television broadcast. Viewers wearing red-and-green glasses suddenly saw rocket ships and ray guns leaping out of their screens, a moment when home entertainment tried to literally step into the third dimension.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

published.match_reason: An experimental 3D sci‑fi TV broadcast parallels the launch of a new, boundary‑bending audio format; both are introductions to immersive, beyond-the-usual sensory experiences.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1985, Space Shuttle Challenger lifted off on mission STS‑51‑B, carrying Spacelab and a small orbiting laboratory of living experiments. Over a week in orbit, astronauts tended to monkeys and laboratory rats, grew crystals, and studied how weightlessness changes life itself—turning the shuttle into a brief floating research station.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: Both the shuttle mission and the orca dives explore life in extreme environments, with calm, cinematic music echoing weightless or underwater, otherworldly motion.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1986, the nuclear‑powered aircraft carrier USS Enterprise became the first ship of its kind to transit the Suez Canal. This massive floating airbase, driven by reactors instead of fuel tanks, slid through a narrow man‑made waterway that links two seas—an encounter between high-tech naval power and one of Earth’s most strategic engineering feats.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A nuclear carrier threading the Suez is an epic sea passage, nicely mirrored by an episode recorded on a boat and built around life on the water and long journeys.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1770, James Cook’s ship Endeavour dropped anchor in a bay on Australia’s east coast, where European maps showed only blank shoreline. Struck by the rich plant life catalogued by botanist Joseph Banks, Cook named it Botany Bay—an early moment when European exploration collided with an already ancient, Indigenous landscape.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: Cook’s coastal landfall, rich in botany and sea imagery, fits a warm episode about seafaring, coasts, and musical voyages across historic waters.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1826, an earthquake struck Italy’s Central Apennines, shaking Rome and Spoleto and damaging the ancient basilica of San Paolo Fuori le Mura. For people used to seeing churches as eternal stone, the sudden cracking of walls and collapse of artwork must have felt like the ground itself withdrawing its promise of stability.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A devastating quake that shakes ancient structures resonates with an episode about weathering storms and finding constancy amid upheaval, set to reflective, hopeful rock and soul.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1991, a magnitude 7.0 earthquake struck Georgia’s Racha region, reaching a destructive intensity of IX on the MSK scale. Entire mountain villages crumpled, churches and towers that had stood for centuries fell, and the landscape itself subtly shifted—an abrupt reminder that tectonic plates move on their own slow, unbothered rhythm.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: The sudden, jolting energy of a major quake pairs with an episode literally themed around electric shock, with high-voltage rock mirroring the earth’s violent release of energy.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1991, one of the deadliest cyclones in modern history slammed into Bangladesh’s Chittagong district with winds around 155 mph. The storm surge swallowed coastal villages, leaving at least 138,000 people dead and up to ten million homeless—an extreme example of how geography, poverty, and climate can combine into catastrophe.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: A brutal natural disaster invites reflection on climate and human vulnerability; the calm, slow-tempo selection offers a meditative counterpoint, a space to breathe and think about a planet under strain.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 2015, a Major League Baseball game between the Baltimore Orioles and Chicago White Sox was played in an eerie silence: officially closed to the public due to unrest in Baltimore, it set the all‑time MLB attendance record of zero. Players competed in a vast, echoing stadium where every crack of the bat and shout in the dugout suddenly felt strangely intimate.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra
songs: The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind

published.match_reason: A silent, crowdless game turns focus to pure sound inside an empty stadium, mirroring an episode about stripping away noise and listening with heightened attention.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1975, as the Vietnam War neared its end, North Vietnamese forces seized all remaining South Vietnamese–held outposts in the Trường Sa (Spratly) Islands. These tiny reefs and islets, barely peeking above the waves, were prized not for their size but for the strategic reach they gave across the South China Sea.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

published.match_reason: Island outposts and contested seas echo an episode that blends geography, travel and seafaring (“Ma come fanno i marinai”), connecting strategic archipelagos with musical landscapes.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1970, U.S. and South Vietnamese troops invaded eastern Cambodia to attack bases along the Ho Chi Minh Trail. The operation spilled the Vietnam War into another country and ignited massive protests back home, as images of widening conflict collided with a generation already questioning power and violence.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: An unpopular war expansion and the protests it sparked align with an episode centered on Rage Against the Machine, whose music channels anti-war anger and political resistance.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1974, amid the Watergate scandal, U.S. President Richard Nixon announced he would release edited transcripts of his secret White House tape recordings. Instead of calming the country, the censored, expletive‑laden pages only deepened suspicion, revealing how much power can hide behind closed doors—and microphones.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

Tracklist:
Boban Marković Orkestar – SAT
Anouar Brahem – The Astounding Eyes of Rita
Kikagaku Moyo – Dripping Sun
Son Lux – Lost It to Trying

published.match_reason: A political scandal that forced a nation to question its assumptions fits an episode about unlearning and re-evaluating beliefs, inviting listeners to “think again” about trust and power.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1992, riots erupted in Los Angeles after a jury acquitted four police officers filmed brutally beating motorist Rodney King. For days, smoke curled above the city as neighborhoods burned, 63 people died, and music, art, and protest began reshaping how the U.S. talked about race, justice, and cameras as witnesses.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

published.match_reason: The LA riots grew from deep racial injustice and policing, themes central to Kendrick Lamar’s work; the episode’s focus on identity and liberation resonates with communities refusing to stay boxed in by systemic violence.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 2013, a powerful explosion ripped through an office building near Prague’s National Theatre, likely caused by a gas leak. The blast shattered windows across the historic center and injured dozens, a sudden collision between invisible infrastructure and fragile urban life.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: A sudden urban disaster born from hidden systems suggests the need to understand the structures around us; a reflective episode on learning and slow progress offers a thoughtful counterpoint about paying attention and adapting.

published.fact_text: Also on April 29, 2013, National Airlines Flight 102, a Boeing 747‑400 freighter, crashed moments after takeoff from Bagram Airfield in Afghanistan. A load of heavy military vehicles shifted backward in the cargo hold, sending the giant aircraft nose‑up and out of control, a stark lesson in how physics and balance quietly rule the skies.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

published.match_reason: A dramatic aviation accident brings raw danger and gravity; the fierce, guitar‑driven energy of women in rock matches the intensity and mechanical power of a 747 pushed past its limits.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1997, the Chemical Weapons Convention entered into force, legally banning the production, stockpiling, and use of chemical weapons for its signatories. For the first time, an entire class of weapons of mass destruction was placed under a global regime of inspections and destruction, an ongoing experiment in whether humanity can collectively un‑invent its worst ideas.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

published.match_reason: A treaty seeking to dismantle instruments of mass suffering aligns with Afrobeat’s history of political critique and calls for freedom, turning from weapons to rhythm as tools for change.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1916, after six intense days of street fighting, the leaders of the Easter Rising in Dublin surrendered to British forces. Their armed rebellion had failed militarily, but the executions that followed helped turn public opinion and planted the seeds of Irish independence in the decades to come.

episode.title: Morning Drops #28 - Under Scottish Skies
episode.description: Gaia shares a personal moment of transition — a shift in seasons, in rhythm, and in life. From this space of quiet change comes a musical journey through Scotland: not the usual names, but voices that speak from the edges. A personal ritual and a small act of curiosity: an invitation to notice what grounds us when everything moves.
songs: Paolo Nutini - Pencil Full of Lead

published.match_reason: An Irish insurrection about identity and self‑determination pairs with an episode devoted to Celtic sounds and shifting northern skies, both steeped in place, history, and quiet resilience.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1862, Union forces under General Henry Halleck began the Siege of Corinth, aiming to capture a small Mississippi railroad town whose crossroads controlled much of the American South’s rail traffic. The battle was as much about logistics and supply lines as bullets—a reminder that in war, tracks and timetables can be as decisive as cannons.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A siege over rail junctions is a conflict about movement and routes; a travel‑themed, gently roaming playlist reframes movement not as conquest but as meditation and perspective shift.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1781, British and French fleets clashed off Martinique in the Battle of Fort Royal. As cannon smoke drifted over Caribbean waters, the fight was as much about who would control colonial trade and sea lanes as about flags and pride—a naval chess match played with creaking wooden giants.

episode.title: Morning Drops #23 - Morning Dubs
episode.description: Recorded while still processing Houghton Festival. One of the mornings there, waking up in the woods to a reggae DJ set — no phone signal, just trees, music, and loved ones. A slow selection of reggae, dub, and morning rhythms. Dubs instead of drops. Start your day slow — with space to breathe.
songs: The Gaylads - Joy in the Morning
King Tubby - King Tubby Meets the Rockers Uptown

published.match_reason: A colonial naval battle in Caribbean waters contrasts with a peaceful, rootsy selection shaped by Caribbean music—reclaiming the same sea lanes with dub echo and morning light instead of cannon fire.
